excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Useful Words in the Philippines." Here are some useful words for the Philippines: I'll share some local words that the models taught me after our photoshoot! [
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Useful Words in the Philippines."

▼Useful Words in the Philippines

I'll share some local words that the models taught me after our photoshoot!

【Kumusta クムスタ】

This means "How are you?" It's the equivalent of "How are you?" in English. It comes from the Spanish "como estas?"

【Salamat サラマット】

This means "Thank you." When you want to express gratitude, using this instead of "Thank you" will make a different impression.

【Oo/ Hindi　オオ/ヒンディ】

Oo　－　Yes

Hindi　－　No

"Are you coming to the party?"

Oo, I'm coming.　"Yes, I'm going."

Hindi, I am not. 　"No, I'm not going."

It's totally OK if it gets mixed with English like this.

Even locals sometimes mix it with English.

【Po ポー】

This is the equivalent of polite expressions like "desu" and "masu" in Japanese. When responding "yes" to someone older than you, saying "Opo" instead of "Oo" sounds more polite.

【Kuya/Ate クヤ/アテ】

These are words used for older siblings, relatives, colleagues, and acquaintances. You call males Kuya (older brother) and females Ate (older sister).

【Tara タラ】

This is similar to the English "Let's go" and is a commonly used phrase when suggesting "Let's do something" or "Let's go somewhere."

【Pwede プウェデ】

This is the equivalent of English "can" or "may," and is commonly used when asking "Can I~?" or "May I~?" and in responses to such questions.

【Magandang araw マガンダン アラウ】

This is a greeting meaning "Good day," equivalent to the English "Good day," and can be used from morning to evening. You can also use it when you want to say "Have a nice day." By the way, "Magandang" means "beautiful~."

There are also other greetings such as:

"Magandang umaga" (マガンダン ウマガ — Good morning)

"Magandang hapon" (マガンダン ハーポン — Good afternoon)

"Magandang gabi" (マガンダン ガビ — Good evening)

【Paalam パアラム】

This is a farewell greeting meaning "Goodbye." It's a somewhat old-fashioned expression, so recently it's more natural to just say "Bye" in English, but foreigners using it will be appreciated.

【Ingat インガット】

This is the equivalent of "Take care" in English. It's used with close friends and colleagues when you want to show concern for someone's well-being or wish them good health.

【Walang anuman ワラン アヌマン】

This means "You're welcome." When someone says "Salamat" or "Thank you," I recommend politely responding with "Walang anuman."

【Masarap マサラップ】

This means "Delicious." If you get to enjoy Filipino cuisine during your trip or stay in the Philippines, try telling the restaurant staff "Masarap."

【Magkano マッカーノ】

This means "How much is it?" When you want to know the price at a shop, try asking "Magkano?" Some Filipinos try to sell overpriced goods or services to foreigners. Be cautious of overly friendly sellers and make sure to compare prices before buying.

【Ano ang pangalan mo? アノン パンガラン モ】

This is a phrase for asking someone "What is your name?" Try asking the name of someone you'd like to get to know.

If you ever go to the Philippines,

definitely try using these!

I use them here and there too!

So that's it for today — a quick Filipino language lesson!

*I used ChatGPT for this one because I wasn't feeling well.

I'll be back writing on my own once I recover!!

title_en: Input for the Sake of Output
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Input for the Sake of Output." ▼Input & Output Beyond English It's been 10 days since I came to Cebu, and I've gotten used to life
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Input for the Sake of Output."

▼Input & Output Beyond English

It's been 10 days since I came to Cebu,

and I've gotten used to life here.

Originally, I'd been living as an address hopper since around November 2021, traveling all around Japan for my work for two years, so staying in the same place for more than 10 days is really rare for me.

During that period of traveling around,

I was meeting all sorts of people

and visiting all sorts of places,

so I had no shortage of things to output.

But when you stay in the same place

and take English classes from 8 AM to 5 PM,

you can do input and output for English,

but getting input on other information

is tough when you're here.

That said, we live in an internet age.

Anyone can access information from anywhere in the world.

In fact, I check daily on the moves of people who are currently doing business globally and from whom I get management hints and lessons.

And if there's something I can apply to myself, I apply it.

"Applying = Output" is something

I was able to make a habit of

starting from my corporate days

and into when I went independent.

But after a long time,

going back to a life where my schedule is fixed and my movement is restricted,

I feel a sense of fear that the quantity and quality of my input and output might drop.

When I was a company employee, my time was restricted,

and now I understand that the quantity and quality of my input and output

were overwhelmingly less compared to freelancers.

Knowing that now, I feel the need to do English input and output

while also using every bit of my remaining time

to focus on the quality of business input and output.

Until now,

I used to do input in a leisurely way,

but with limited time,

if I don't produce quality output,

I can't run my business remotely,

and besides, I need to share different discoveries and lessons every day in this 3-month Cebu activity journal.

Last year's cycling around Taiwan had me riding from morning to evening every day and attending social gatherings at night,

and since I was riding with different people every day

and sleeping in different places,

the constantly changing environment

made it easy to produce output.

But this time it's not like that,

which I've only just now realized.

To avoid situations like

"Oh no, I've got nothing to write about, what do I do,"

I'll pay attention to the finer details

and deliver content that's entertaining

for all of you who are following along.

For the first time in about 2 years, I bought toilet paper and deodorizing spray myself lol

First time since I lived alone!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Valentine's Day." ▼Valentine's Day in the Philippines February 14th is Valentine's Day. In Japan, Valentine's Day is when women approach men, but this custom is unique to Japan.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Valentine's Day."

▼Valentine's Day in the Philippines

February 14th is Valentine's Day.

In Japan, Valentine's Day is when women approach men, but this custom is unique to Japan.

#It started with a Valentine's sale by Isetan department store, which ran ads and campaigns saying "Give chocolate for Valentine's Day."

In the Philippines,

it's customary for both men and women to give bouquets and chocolate.

I'd casually heard about this,

but at today's cafeteria, the teachers handed out flowers and chocolate to the students.

The students include Japanese, Taiwanese, Chinese, and Korean people,

and since it was a first for everyone, they were all taking videos.

*I was one of them.

Check out this rare look at Valentine's Day during study abroad in Cebu!

https://youtu.be/SM2h-fThy5M?si=ZwFEsjIbrTd8nT0S

If you watched it, you can see —

the combo of guitar and music, plus flowers and chocolate! lol

Filipinos tend to be cheerful people,

but today especially, everyone was in high spirits all day!

Filipinos love romantic things.

Seeing that essence come through at events like this was a great experience.

Actually, today is also the 3rd anniversary of the masu brand "FOMUS," but I'll save that story for another time!

Since this is the Cebu activity journal, today's post is mainly about Cebu's Valentine's culture with videos, but I hope you found it fun!

The flowers and chocolate I received

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Repeating Experiments." ▼February Is All About Experimenting! The second weekend break of my Cebu life is coming up soon. In the first week, I was able to do Masu Photo shoots with locals without any problems, and
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Repeating Experiments."

▼February Is All About Experimenting!

The second weekend break of my Cebu life is coming up soon.

In the first week, I was able to do Masu Photo shoots with locals

without any problems,

and I got a feel for how to attract participants,

so in the second week, I won't be doing additional Masu Photo shoot recruitment.

Instead, I'm planning to push forward with my other business.

Actually, on May 3rd and 4th, 2024,

the masu brand "FOMUS" has decided to hold a digital art exhibition

at the Cultural Center in Sakado City, Saitama Prefecture.

We're already gathering works,

but since I'm here, I thought it'd be great to have people in Cebu create pieces to display too!

So I made a new flyer.

This time, I'll be holding an "AI Art Seminar" here — something I've been doing for the past two years.

Yes. Some of you might recognize this — it's designed to look like the "Masu Photo shoot" ad I put out a week ago.

Here are the details:

Beginners Welcome!

Would you like to display your illustrations at an art exhibition in Japan?

Even if you're new to illustration, you can create with confidence using the latest AI technology that allows you to draw with words. No need for pens or ink!

We are hosting an art exhibition in Japan in May 2024 and are calling for submissions from around the world!

Schedule:

February 17th 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM

2:00 PM to 3:00 PM

3:00 PM to 4:00 PM

February 18th 1:00 PM to 2:00 PM

2:00 PM to 3:00 PM

3:00 PM to 4:00 PM

(Up to 3 participants per session) *Friends and family are welcome to join

(Each session lasts 1 hour)

So it's set up like this to reach anyone who [if there are any] wants to create work over the two days.

This time, I wasn't feeling well,

so I wasn't sure if I could hold it over the weekend,

but I felt a lot better today,

so I decided it was doable

and released the ad on Instagram.

I put it out on Thursday, February 15, 2024 around 7 PM,

and I'm planning to run it

until around 10 PM on Friday, February 16, 2024.

That said, the cost is only about 1,000 yen per day.

Stay tuned for the next post to see how it goes!

▼AI Art Experiments

In preparation for holding an AI illustration seminar in Cebu,

I had a friend from my language school

be a test participant.

What we did was the same as usual,

so nothing special really lol

Here's what we actually created:

Since this piece was created in the Philippines,

I'd love to display it somewhere.

(Since it was a free test, it'll be A4 size though.)

When it comes to connecting AI art with masu, the card game "SILVA" is the representative FOMUS content, but going forward, I'm thinking of doing new things with "AI Art x FOMUS," so having more people who can do AI art is great for us too.

So,

this weekend, I'll be holding an AI art class in Cebu (tentatively)!!

Well, let's see how it goes.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "The System of Graduation Ceremonies." ▼There's a Graduation Ceremony Every Week Today is Friday. Every weekend, a "graduation ceremony" is held for those finishing their time at the school that week.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "The System of Graduation Ceremonies."

▼There's a Graduation Ceremony Every Week

Today is Friday.

Every weekend, a "graduation ceremony" is held for those finishing their time at the school that week.

I think other schools besides mine do this too.

Several of the graduates give speeches,

and senior school staff also give speeches.

Then at the end, there's a photo session and a social gathering.

It's set up so you can say goodbye and thank the people who've helped you.

Also, even people who aren't graduating can attend and watch.

In my case, I'm staying for 12 weeks,

but the people who started at the same time as me and became my friends

are confirmed to be graduating every week starting this week.

The last time I participated in a graduation ceremony

was back in college,

so it felt like it had been quite a while.

I'll be graduating (tentatively) in 10 weeks too,

so maybe I'll feel something at that time.

I want to make sure to write down those feelings when the time comes.

▼New Students Arrive Every Week Too

Where there's a graduation ceremony, there's also an entrance ceremony.

New students come in every week.

Because this cycle of enrollment and graduation keeps running,

the school can keep accepting students.

I was impressed that their recruitment system works so well, but on the other hand,

I felt like they don't quite have a system designed to make graduates remember the school

and become "fans."

I mean, I graduated from university too, but I'm not exactly a fan of my university, so it's hard to say.

But if there were some way for graduates to stay involved,

wouldn't that create new monetization opportunities?

That's what I was thinking,

but language schools are already doing well financially,

so maybe they don't need to think about that.

*In a way, it's already systematized.

The masu brand "FOMUS" doesn't have

a system like enrollment and graduation,

and I don't plan to create one,

but I did feel that I need to build some kind of system

that keeps customer acquisition running continuously.

If you look deeper into the business model of language schools,

I bet there's a lot more to uncover.

Well, I doubt many people are that interested,

but it seems fun to look at it from a different perspective.

So that's today's story about checking out a graduation ceremony!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Things That Didn't Go Well." ▼I Was Actually Feeling Unwell It's Saturday. The language school is closed. So it's a day I can move around freely.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Things That Didn't Go Well."

▼I Was Actually Feeling Unwell

It's Saturday.　The language school is closed.

So it's a day I can move around freely.

Actually, I've been feeling unwell since around last Sunday.

I didn't take my temperature, but I had a fever at night,

and on top of that, I had stomach pain every day.

Even so, I had classes every day,

so I was studying English while holding my stomach.

Yes, my stomach was a wreck.

I've been to about 14 countries so far,

and I'd never had stomach problems overseas before,

so this was my first time getting hit.

When I thought about what caused it —

maybe the food didn't agree with me,

or maybe it was from gargling with tap water —

something got to me.

Thanks to daily Biofermin,

plus Pocari Sweat and Yakult that I bought at the supermarket, I've fully recovered now.

But this week was pretty rough,

and I wasn't sure if I could do Masu Photo shoots,

so I had planned to rest this weekend until I fully recovered.

However, since I bounced back,

I thought about doing an illustration seminar using image-generating AI — my other business besides masu — something I can do indoors.

I'd been running Instagram ads since Thursday,

but the number of people messaging me dropped dramatically,

and in the end, nobody actually wanted to give it a try.

The thing is, the culture of "creating images with AI" isn't established yet — not in Japan, and not in the Philippines either, as of February 2024. People can't imagine it, which is probably why there were so few offers.

I realize my judgment was off on this,

and I feel I should have started recruiting earlier.

*I learned that the vast majority of people can't imagine how these are made

▼AI Is Still a High Hurdle

On top of that,

I feel the barrier to this kind of content is quite high.

Not many Filipinos interact with AI on a daily basis.

*Same goes for Japanese people.

So I also learned that unless it's straightforward content like Masu Photo — "hold a masu and take a photo" — people won't come.

Next weekend, I'm planning to do Masu Photo shoots again.

As for AI art,

I felt the best approach would be to create together with foreign students at the language school.

*That way, I just need to talk to them and have them say some words, and it works.

That's how I confirmed that AI content hasn't really spread yet — I got that data.

The fact that Masu Photo succeeded

while the AI art seminar didn't work out

was a discovery for me too,

and I find it quite interesting.

Also, the week after next (the 4th week of February),

I'm thinking of putting out an ad targeting store owners who would use FOMUS masu, asking "Can we do something with masu?"

The cost of getting data is a necessary expense.

With all that in mind,

tonight, I headed to a nameless bar about a 15-minute walk from the school.

While enjoying conversation with friends,

I also promoted the FOMUS brand.

It's a pretty low-key activity,

but these kinds of moves are necessary too.

And that was my second Saturday in Cebu!

https://youtu.be/GfHvwxSByUo?si=pseTqiEnVh1eCxnQ

title_en: A Normal Day Off in Cebu
excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "A Normal Day Off in Cebu." ▼I Tried Having a Normal Day Off It's been exactly 2 weeks since I came to Cebu. Today, the language school is closed. And since I didn't schedule any FOMUS
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "A Normal Day Off in Cebu."

▼I Tried Having a Normal Day Off

It's been exactly 2 weeks since I came to Cebu.

Today, the language school is closed.

And since I didn't schedule any FOMUS Masu Photo shoots, I went around Cebu city with some friends I made here.

When I go overseas, I always visit a spa.

Spas in Japan are expensive everywhere, but in Southeast Asia, they're considerably cheaper and the quality is decent.

So,

off I went!

The place I visited was called "Tinder Spa."

I got a 90-minute "Hot Oil Massage" for 800 pesos.

My friend got a 90-minute "Dry Massage" for 600 pesos.

Converting to Japanese yen, mine was about 2,200 yen.

In Japan, a 90-minute oil massage

would easily cost over 10,000 yen,

but here it's less than a quarter of that.

And the quality was pretty good too!!

My masseuse was a man,

and he did a thorough job.

As for spas in Cebu,

I thought it'd be great to go to one after a photoshoot next time.

After that, we headed to IT Park.

I'd been to Cebu IT Park once last week for the Masu Photo shoot, so I had a rough idea of the place, but today I took my time walking around.

On the open floor,

there was some kind of dance event for young people.

There were a ton of spectators,

and I could see that as entertainment in the Philippines, it was generating real excitement.

"I'd love to do a Masu Photo shoot with those dancers~"

That's how I felt.

If I could somehow connect with them in the next 10 weeks

and make it to a Masu Photo shoot, that would be amazing...

I'll look into this a bit more.

The spa was done, I watched a bit of the dance event,

and with nothing left to do, I thought about heading back, but...

Then I remembered that IT Park has a movie theater,

so I went to check if anything was showing.

Turns out, a movie was starting in 30 minutes.

That movie was "MADAME WEB" —

the latest Marvel film,

which doesn't start showing in Japan until next week.

I just felt like

"Watching a movie in Cebu"

was an experience worth having,

so I paid 290 pesos (650 yen) and went in.

Of course, there were no Japanese subtitles.

Even so, thanks to my effort to listen hard to the English and the quality of the film, I understood less than 50% of the words but thoroughly enjoyed the story.

I never get the chance to watch a full 2-hour English movie without subtitles, so in a way, it might have been a good use of money for English practice.

After that, we went to the night market at IT Park.

There were tons of stalls lined up,

and we grabbed food there for dinner.

Then at a billiards bar nearby,

I didn't play billiards but had a beer

And then took a Grab back to school.

And just like that,

I spent a day in Cebu.

Spa → Event → Movie → Street food dinner → Bar

It was great to have such an entertainment-packed day!

That said, I can't keep living like this forever (I need to spread FOMUS), so this was just a chronicle of experiencing "a normal day off during language school."

Next week, I'll be having a meetup with locals and doing Masu Photo shoots.

Check out the full rundown of today on YouTube!

A Working Adult's Study Abroad in Cebu — Day Off Log

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Eating Balut." ▼Switching Up Teachers It's the 3rd week of my Cebu life. I'm facing the question of how to make the most of the remaining 10 weeks at the language school for English training, and
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Eating Balut."

▼Switching Up Teachers

It's the 3rd week of my Cebu life.

I'm facing the question of how to make the most of the remaining 10 weeks at the language school for English training, and I'm currently using my swap slots to trade teachers in search of ones that suit me best.

Being able to do this is a perk of language study abroad.

Of course, it's not that I dislike them as people —

it's about teaching style and compatibility.

I've been changing at least one teacher every week,

and I noticed there's a benefit to this.

That is, every time I get a new teacher,

there's a self-introduction session.

In my case, since business is the ultimate goal,

how clearly I can explain myself during the introduction is super important.

With teachers I've already been taking classes with, there's no occasion for a fresh introduction,

but with newly swapped teachers,

there is a self-introduction, so I can try various approaches.

"What phrasing gets through to the other person?"

"How about this word?"

"Is this being communicated correctly?"

There's a lot to think about,

but thinking of it as self-introduction experiments,

it works out quite well.

In my case, since I'm here to spread the masu brand "FOMUS,"

I talk about FOMUS, share its story,

and describe my own activities.

It turns out to be a good balance,

and people seem to find it interesting.

Being able to refine my self-introduction in English

is something I'll absolutely need for future activities,

so being able to edit it here is great.

Also, the teacher in charge of my writing class

has already stopped opening the writing textbook

and switched to a conversation-style format

to strengthen my speaking and listening.

After 3 weeks of continuing,

they understand what I need,

and they're supporting me with a focus on what's most important for me right now.

This isn't something that could happen in the first week,

but being able to gradually find the right study methods and teachers is something I'm glad about.

▼Going Out for Drinks at Night

Now here's the main topic for today!

After classes, I ate at the school,

and then headed to a bar about a 5-minute walk away.

It was a bar the teachers told me about today,

and I went right away.

*I've got the action-taking spirit.

While drinking beer,

I bought some eggs from a nearby street stall.

And get this!

They weren't raw or hard-boiled eggs —

they were eggs boiled at a partially developed stage,

which is a local food here.

It's called "balut."

You don't really get the chance

to eat a developed egg in Japan,

so it was a fun experience.

https://youtu.be/au0hJEfkbms?si=zcYDLAhBAj13f7NE

*Just a video of me eating eggs, that's it

Experiences like this that you can only have locally

are super fun.

And with that, I'll keep learning English this week

while enjoying the culture.

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "Real Business English." ▼Online Meeting So. Today, I had an online meeting related to the masu brand "FOMUS."
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "Real Business English."

▼Online Meeting

So.

Today, I had an online meeting related to the masu brand "FOMUS."

I won't go into the details of the meeting here,

but there were about 40 people in the group,

and I was the only Japanese person there.

There were two meetings.

One was a lecture-style session,

so I just had to listen.

The other meeting involved

giving a self-introduction and presenting an overview of my business.

Everything was in English.

After finishing classes at the language school in Cebu,

I had these two meetings,

and I felt like I was able to catch a bit more than before.

That said, I'm in a position where I need to be able to handle

business-level English conversation,

so being able to catch just a little

isn't nearly enough yet...

During the self-introduction part,

I briefly talked about the masu brand "FOMUS."

In English, of course.

As the leader of FOMUS,

introducing and explaining our brand in English is something that will only increase going forward.

Honestly, all the other entrepreneurs and investors around me were fluent in English,

and I was barely getting by.

Plus, this isn't the kind of work where you can bring a translator,

so I had no choice but to manage on my own. But since I've been immersed in English lately, it helped.

However,

with my current English ability,

I haven't yet gained enough language skills to bring the brand "FOMUS" overseas.

I need to study English much, much more

and clear this language barrier —

that feeling grew even stronger.

And going forward, I'll be using English even more

to approach entrepreneurs and investors,

so these online meetings with people from around the world

gave me a major wake-up call.

I'm really starting to feel that FOMUS's overseas battle is getting closer!

I'll keep studying hard!

excerpt_en: Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS! Today I'll be talking about "I Started a Morning Routine." ▼The Early Bird Catches the Worm It's been 17 days since I came to Cebu. Being here, time feels like it flies by.
body_en:
Hey there, it's MaSU from FOMUS!

Today I'll be talking about "I Started a Morning Routine."

▼The Early Bird Catches the Worm

It's been 17 days since I came to Cebu.

Being here, time feels like it flies by.

Every morning I wake up at 7, get ready,

and classes start at 8.

Lunchtime is at noon.

Afternoon classes start at 1 PM,

and classes end at 5 PM.

Dinner is from 6 PM.

After that is free time,

and in my case, evening free time

is my serious business time, or time for gathering information, and I'd been going to bed around midnight to 1 AM

for these past 2 weeks, but...

Recently,

a completely separate goal came up.

That is, the "Medoc Marathon."

https://www.marathondumedoc.com/en/registration-global/

It's a marathon held every summer in France,

famous for things like

"running in cosplay"

and "the water stations serve wine."

Since starting the masu brand "FOMUS,"

losing my home and starting life as an address hopper, the people I meet and interact with changed dramatically.

And one common trait among people who are truly enjoying life

that I've noticed over these 3 years is

"they have stamina."

This is true across people in their 20s through 60s.

They're not necessarily muscular,

but they have healthy bodies!!

I'm not overweight by any means,

but I've been relying on my youth and haven't done any fitness training. *I do take care of my appearance though.

In a few years I'll be in my 30s,

and from then on, "youth" will fade

and it'll become a stamina game.

No matter how good your business brain is,

if you don't have stamina, you'll tire out faster, which means less time spent on work.

That's why people with stamina

can dedicate more time to their activities

and are living their best lives right now.

What I find even more interesting is that

they're all "drinkers."

The people around me say

they run so they can drink.

That might be one reason,

but overall, what I think is that they deeply understand that "your body is your most fundamental asset."

It's hard to realize this in your 20s,

but thankfully, I'm surrounded by seniors in their 30s and older, and they're all working incredibly hard.

There's no guaranteed path to success,

but there is a guaranteed path to failure,

and one of them is "being unhealthy" — that's what I believe.

Which means,

living a healthy lifestyle is extremely important for doing business, and starting now is nothing but upside.

And back to the Medoc Marathon —

registration is about to open soon.

This year it's being held on September 7th.

Of course, I don't know if I'll definitely get in,

but I'm going to apply.

Plus, this year there are also the Paris Olympics,

and on top of that,

I'm planning to travel around Europe starting in July.

In other words, the Medoc Marathon in France would be a bit more cost-effective than going from Japan!! (Though the accommodation costs are no joke.)

So,

with all these conditions aligned,

I want to run the Medoc Marathon and build healthy lifestyle habits —

and so...

I decided to run every morning!

Luckily,

the school has a treadmill,

so I wake up at 6,

start running at 6:30,

finish after 7,

and get ready for classes at 8.

I actually tried it, and it feels really good!!

To make sure I'm ready to run outside every day

when I return to Japan in May,

I'll keep at it steadily.

I've got another goal here in Cebu.

It's a small goal,

but I'm going to keep chipping away at it!
